ScopeThe methodThe five stepsWhat we cannot account forWhere to look
/sources/

Sources & databases

The record only holds because it points outward. These are the sources we reason against — grouped by the question they help answer.

Yad Vashem — Names Database ↗
victims
Answers: Who owned this book?
international
USHMM — Survivors & Victims Database ↗
victims
Answers: Who owned this book?
international
Bundesarchiv Gedenkbuch ↗
victims
Answers: Who was the owner, and their fate?
Germany
Looted Cultural Assets ↗
victims
Answers: Whose bookplate is this?
Germany
JewishGen — Yizkor & community lists ↗
victims
Answers: Which community, and who lived there?
Eastern Europe
OAD monthly reports (NARA / fold3) ↗
shipping
Answers: Was it sent here, and when?
Germany / U.S.
OAD list — 582 Jewish libraries (Sep 1946) ↗
shipping
Answers: Which institution held it? (58,667 books)
Germany
OAD list — 35 libraries (Jul 1946) ↗
shipping
Answers: Which institution held it? (33,499 books)
Germany
“Privately-owned Book Collections,” Wiesbaden (1949) ↗
shipping
Answers: Whose private library was it?
Germany
Freidus Classification Shelf List (Dropsie) ↗
bibliographic
Answers: Was it sourced as a JCR gift?
Philadelphia
Rare-manuscripts accession list (Dropsie) ↗
bibliographic
Answers: Did the manuscript come from JCR?
Philadelphia
Bencowitz, Library Markings (1946), NLI ↗
reference
Answers: What stamp is this?
Offenbach
Provenance research & text: Bruce Nielsen · Penn Libraries, Katz Center for Advanced Judaic StudiesUncertainty in this record is shown, not hidden.
